content: Identify the element so that the last electron enters in ( $n-2$ )f sub shell.
explanation: The ( $\mathrm{n}-2$ )f subshell corresponds to the f-block elements - lanthanides ( $4 f$ ) and actinides ( $5 f$ ). Amongst given, only No $(\mathrm{Z}=102)$ is f - block element with electronic configuration $[\mathrm{Rn}] 5 \mathrm{f}^{14} 6 \mathrm{~d}^0 7 \mathrm{~s}^2$, while other elements " $\mathrm{Au}, \mathrm{Hg}$, and W" belong to $\mathrm{d}-$ block.

content: What is the number of unpaired electrons in $\mathrm{C}_0$ in +2 state?
explanation:
The outer electronic configuration for Co ( $\mathrm{Z}=27$ ) in +2 state can be given as, $\mathrm{Co}^{+2}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^7 4 \mathrm{~s}^0$

$$ \therefore \quad \text { Total number of unpaired electrons }=3 $$

The transition elements ( $\mathrm{Hf}, \mathrm{Mo}, \mathrm{Zr}$ ) belong to d-block of the modern periodic table while the inner transition element (No) belongs to f-block of the modern periodic table. Nobelium $(\mathrm{Z}=102)=[\mathrm{Rn}] 5 \mathrm{f}^{14} 6 \mathrm{~d}^0 7 \mathrm{~s}^2$.

content: Which from following metal ions in their respective oxidation states forms colourless compounds?
explanation:
Let us analyze each option using electronic configuration and the concept of $d$-block and color formation:

Key Concept:

Transition metal ions form coloured compounds due to $d$–d electronic transitions. If an ion has no electrons in its $d$-orbital ($d^0$), or a completely filled $d$-subshell ($d^{10}$), they generally form colourless compounds.

Option A: $\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}$

  • Atomic number of Ti = 22

  • Electronic configuration of Ti: $[Ar]\ 4s^{2} 3d^{2}$

  • $\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}$ loses 4 electrons: $2$ from $4s$ and $2$ from $3d$

  • So, $\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}: [Ar]\ 3d^0$

  • $d^0$ configuration $\to$ no $d$–$d$ transitions possible

  • Forms colourless compounds

Option B: $\mathrm{V}^{3+}$

  • Atomic number of V = 23

  • Electronic configuration of V: $[Ar]\ 4s^2 3d^3$

  • $\mathrm{V}^{3+}$ loses 3 electrons: $2$ from $4s$, $1$ from $3d$

  • $\mathrm{V}^{3+}: [Ar]\ 3d^2$

  • $d^2$ configuration $\to$ $d$–$d$ transitions possible

  • Forms coloured compounds

Option C: $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$

  • Atomic number of Cu = 29

  • Electronic configuration of Cu: $[Ar]\ 4s^1 3d^{10}$

  • $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ loses 2 electrons: $1$ from $4s$, $1$ from $3d$

  • $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}: [Ar]\ 3d^9$

  • $d^9$ configuration $\to$ $d$–$d$ transitions possible

  • Forms coloured compounds

Option D: $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$

  • Atomic number of Mn = 25

  • Electronic configuration of Mn: $[Ar]\ 4s^2 3d^5$

  • $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ loses 2 electrons: both from $4s$

  • $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}: [Ar]\ 3d^5$

  • $d^5$ configuration $\to$ $d$–$d$ transitions possible

  • Forms coloured compounds

Final Answer:

Option A $\boxed{\mathrm{Ti}^{4+}}$ forms colourless compounds.

options.content: It has no unpaired electron either in observed or in expected electronic configuration.
It has lowest density amongst all 3d elements.
It has highest ionization enthalpy amongst 3d elements.

explanation:
Observed and expected electronic configuration of Zn is $[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{10} 4 \mathrm{~s}^2$ (no unpaired electrons). $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\left(3 \mathrm{~d}^{10}\right)$ has no unpaired electrons hence its magnetic moment is zero.

Zn has the highest ionisation energy and Sc has the lowest density among the 3 d transition elements.

content: Which element from following does NOT exhibit magnetic moment in +1 state?
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Zn}^{+} \Rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{10} 4 \mathrm{~s}^1 \\ & \mathrm{Co}^{+} \Rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^7 4 \mathrm{~s}^1 \\ & \mathrm{Cu}^{+} \Rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{10} \\ & \mathrm{Mn}^{+} \Rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^5 4 \mathrm{~s}^1 \end{aligned} $$

All ions have unpaired electrons, except $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}$.

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{Cu}^{+}$does not exhibit magnetic moment.

options.content: As per IUPAC convention, transition metal atom have incomplete d-subshell.
These elements are placed in group 3 to 12 in modern periodic table.
In these ( $\mathrm{n}-1$ ) d-orbital is filled successively.

explanation:
Option B is false.

6d‐series means the d-orbitals with principal quantum number 6 (i.e. n–1 = 6 implies n = 7), so these are the d-block elements of the 7th period.

In the modern periodic table, the 7th-period d-block runs from

$$Z=104\;(\text{Rf})\quad\text{to}\quad Z=112\;(\text{Cn}).$$

Elements Ac (Z=89) to Lr (Z=103) belong to the actinide (7f-series), not the 6d-series.

Therefore, “6d-series includes all elements from Ac (Z=89) to Cn (Z=112)” is incorrect.
